perturbation.original_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: This modification changes the non-compete period from three years to one year. This directly contradicts Section 2.02(b), which states the seller will keep confidential information for a period of three years after the execution of this agreement. Now, the agreement states the seller cannot compete for one year, but must keep Confidential Information for three years, creating an uncertainty about the link between non-compete and confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and trade secrets of the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement "trade secrets" means any information that derives independent value from being secret. Confidential Information shall not include any information or documents that (i) are or become publicly available without breach of this Section 2.02, (ii) Seller receives from any third party who, to the best of Seller's knowledge upon reasonable inquiry, is not breaching an obligation of confidence with Purchasers or Parent (including such an obligation under an agreement assigned to Purchasers pursuant to the Asset Sale Agreement) or without an accompanying obligation of confidence, or (iii) is required to be released by law. In the event that Seller is requested in any court or governmental proceeding to disclose any Confidential Information, Seller shall give Purchasers and Parent prompt notice of such request, such that Purchasers or Parent may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief, and in any such proceeding Seller will disclose only so much of the Confidential Information as is required to be disclosed. (b) Seller will keep confidential and will not for a period of three (3) years after the execution of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, divulge to anyone, use or otherwise appropriate any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose whatsoever except to authorized representatives of Purchasers or the Parent.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 2.03 stated a three-year non-compete period. The changed text reduces this to one year. Section 2.02(b) requires the seller to keep confidential information for three years after the agreement's execution. The change to a one-year non-compete period directly contradicts the three-year confidentiality obligation in 2.02(b). This is an inconsistency as defined in the perturbation definition.

perturbation.original_text: 1.04 "TERRITORY" shall mean the United States of America and Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.04 "TERRITORY" shall mean the United States of America.
perturbation.explanation: By removing Canada from the definition of "TERRITORY", the scope of the non-compete agreement is now limited to only the US. This contradicts Section 2.03, which states that the seller will not Compete with the Purchasers 'within the Territory.' Now the territory excludes Canada, meaning the seller is free to compete in Canada after the change, conflicting with the intention for the Seller to be restrained in Canada as of the original contract's terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of "TERRITORY" by removing Canada, while section 2.03 prevents the Seller from competing in the Business within the defined TERRITORY. The removal of Canada creates a contradiction as the seller is now free to compete in Canada after the change, conflicting with the original contract's intention to restrain the seller in Canada, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 2.05 REMEDIES. Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are not an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to an injunction, without the necessity of posting any bond, enjoining, prohibiting, and restraining Seller from the continuance of any such violation, in addition to any monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, including without limitation specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.05 REMEDIES. Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, including without limitation specific performance.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the text to state that 'damages are an adequate remedy', and removing the portion about Purchasers and Parent being entitled to an injunction, this contradicts the implied understanding in section 2.05 from the original text that injunctive relief is an appropriate response to breaches of Article II. This creates an in-text contradiction because the original agreement indicates injunctive relief is preferred due to irreparable harm, but the modification suggests monetary damages are sufficient, affecting how violations of the agreement can be resolved.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.05 REMEDIES. Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, including without limitation specific performance.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters a key element of the remedies available to the Purchasers and Parent. The original text states that 'damages are not an adequate remedy' and that Purchasers and Parent are entitled to an injunction. The changed text states that 'damages are an adequate remedy' and removes the portion about injunctive relief. This creates a direct conflict and inconsistency regarding the remedies available in the case of a violation of Article II, which is a strong contradiction. The changed text and contradicted text are in the same location, but the changes cause a strong enough difference to provide a "YES".

perturbation.original_text: 3.01 SUCCESSORS AND ASSIGNS. This Agreement may not be assigned by any party hereto, except that Purchasers' and Parent's rights, duties and obligations under this Agreement may be assigned and delegated to any subsidiary of Purchasers or Parent or to the acquiror of Purchasers, Parent or the Business in the event either Purchaser or Parent is merged, acquired, sells substantially all of its interest in the Business, or transfers its interest in the Business to any other entity. From and after any such assignment by Purchasers or Parent, the term "Purchasers" or "Parent," as the case may be, shall mean such assignee, and such assignee may enforce any of the rights of Purchasers or Parent under this Agreement as if it were the original party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.01 SUCCESSORS AND ASSIGNS. This Agreement may not be assigned by any party hereto, except that Purchasers' and Parent's rights, duties and obligations under this Agreement may be assigned and delegated to any subsidiary of Purchasers or Parent or to the acquiror of Purchasers, Parent or the Business in the event either Purchaser or Parent is merged, acquired, sells substantially all of its interest in the Business, or transfers its interest in the Business to any other entity. From and after any such assignment by Purchasers or Parent, the term "Purchasers" or "Parent," as the case may be, shall mean such assignee, and such assignee may enforce any of the rights of Purchasers or Parent under this Agreement as if it were the original party hereto. This agreement can only be assigned with written consent from all involved parties.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'This agreement can only be assigned with written consent from all involved parties.' to the end of section 3.01, the definition of successors and assigns is now contradictory with its previous definition. The addition contradicts the existing clause which allows Purchasers' and Parent's rights to be assigned under certain conditions, without explicitly requiring Seller's consent. This creates uncertainty: does the assignment require explicit consent from all parties or only meet the condition of Purchasers' and Parents' rights?
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.01 SUCCESSORS AND ASSIGNS. This Agreement may not be assigned by any party hereto, except that Purchasers' and Parent's rights, duties and obligations under this Agreement may be assigned and delegated to any subsidiary of Purchasers or Parent or to the acquiror of Purchasers, Parent or the Business in the event either Purchaser or Parent is merged, acquired, sells substantially all of its interest in the Business, or transfers its interest in the Business to any other entity. From and after any such assignment by Purchasers or Parent, the term "Purchasers" or "Parent," as the case may be, shall mean such assignee, and such assignee may enforce any of the rights of Purchasers or Parent under this Agreement as if it were the original party hereto. This agreement can only be assigned with written consent from all involved parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a requirement for written consent from all parties for assignment, which directly contradicts the earlier part of section 3.01 that allows Purchasers' and Parent's rights to be assigned under certain conditions (merger, acquisition, etc.) without explicitly stating the need for all parties' consent. This creates a clear inconsistency regarding the assignment process.

perturbation.original_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing 3 <PAGE> 4 Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing 3 <PAGE> 4 Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity. However, after one year, the seller may solicit employees as long as they are not in a managerial role.
perturbation.explanation: Section 2.04 originally restricts solicitation for one year, but the added sentence allows solicitation after one year if the employee is not managerial. This addition contradicts the first sentence by implying a restriction on soliciting managerial employees after the first year. Now, there is uncertainty whether soliciting managerial roles after the year is a violation or not, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing 3 <PAGE> 4 Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity. However, after one year, the seller may solicit employees as long as they are not in a managerial role.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 2.04 to initially restrict solicitation for three years, but then allows solicitation after one year for non-managerial employees. The original phrasing implies a complete ban, while the modification introduces an exception. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as it is unclear whether soliciting managerial employees after one year is permissible or prohibited, making the first part of the clause contradictory to the second part and triggers an Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction. Furthermore, the contradicted text is the same as the changed text and since there is a strong in-text contradiction based on the explanation given, we can safely determine that it is a "YES".
